questions.question: Explain the difference between forward chaining and backward chaining. Which one is data-driven and which is goal-driven?
keyPoints: Forward chaining starts from known facts and derives new facts until goal is reached
Backward chaining starts from the goal and works backwards to find supporting facts
Forward chaining is data-driven (starts with input data)
Backward chaining is goal-driven (starts with desired conclusion)

questions.question: What is unification in FOL? Explain with an example showing successful unification.
keyPoints: Unification finds substitution making two literals identical
Variables can be replaced with constants or other terms
Example: Likes(x, pizza) and Likes(John, y) unify with {x/John, y/pizza}
Failure occurs when conflict exists (e.g., f(x) = g(y))

questions.question: Define Horn clause and explain why Horn clauses are important for practical reasoning systems.
keyPoints: Horn clause has at most one positive literal
Form: (P1 ∧ P2 ∧ ... ∧ Pn) → Q
Enables efficient forward/backward chaining
Basis for Prolog and expert systems
Simplifies computation compared to general clauses

questions.question: Explain Universal Instantiation and Existential Instantiation with examples.
keyPoints: Universal Instantiation: From ∀x P(x), derive P(c) for any constant c
Existential Instantiation: From ∃x P(x), derive P(c) for new constant c
Example: ∀x Dog(x)→Animal(x), Dog(Rex) → Animal(Rex)
UI can be applied repeatedly; EI introduces new unique constant

questions.question: What is the Resolution Principle? Explain how it is used for proof by refutation.
keyPoints: Resolution is a refutation-complete inference rule
To prove KB ⊨ Goal, assume ¬Goal and add to KB
Apply resolution to derive new clauses
Derivation of empty clause (□) proves unsatisfiability
Empty clause means contradiction → original goal is entailed

questions.question: Differentiate between axioms and theorems in knowledge representation.
keyPoints: Axioms are basic facts/rules assumed true without proof
Theorems are statements derived from axioms through inference
Axioms form the foundation of the knowledge base
Theorems are proven based on axioms and inference rules
Axioms represent primitive truths; theorems represent derived truths

questions.question: Explain Modus Ponens and Modus Tollens inference rules with FOL examples.
keyPoints: Modus Ponens: From P→Q and P, infer Q. Example: ∀x (Student(x)→Learns(x)), Student(John) → Learns(John)
Modus Tollens: From P→Q and ¬Q, infer ¬P. Example: ∀x (Bird(x)→Flies(x)), ¬Flies(Tweety) → ¬Bird(Tweety)
Both are fundamental rules used in automated reasoning

questions.question: Consider the domain of a family relationships. Represent the following in FOL: (a) Every parent is a grandparent of everyone else (b) Every family has a mother (c) John is the father of Mary. Then show how forward chaining can derive 'John is a grandparent'.
keyPoints: (a) ∀x ∀y (Parent(x,y) → Grandparent(x,z)) - need proper formalization
Proper representation: ∀x ∀y ∀z (Parent(x,y) ∧ Different(y,z) → Grandparent(x,z))
(b) ∃x (Mother(x) ∧ Family(x)) or similar
(c) Father(John, Mary) with appropriate predicate definitions
Forward chaining: Given Father(John, Mary) and ∀x∀y (Father(x,y)→Parent(x,y)) derive Parent(John, Mary)
Then using ∀x∀y (Parent(x,y)→Grandparent(x,z)) derive Grandparent(John, someone)

questions.question: Explain the knowledge engineering process for building a FOL knowledge base. Discuss the steps involved in going from domain analysis to automated reasoning.
keyPoints: 1. Identify the domain and scope of knowledge
2. Determine important vocabulary (predicates, functions, constants)
3. Encode general knowledge about the domain (axioms)
4. Encode specific problem instances as ground facts
5. Pose queries to the inference engine
6. Debug the knowledge base
Key considerations: Completeness, consistency, decidability
Common pitfalls: Missing axioms, circular definitions, unintended models

questions.question: Describe the steps to convert a FOL sentence to clause form for resolution. Convert the following to clause form: ∀x (P(x) → Q(x))
keyPoints: Steps: Remove implications, Move negations inward, Standardize variables, Remove existential quantifiers (Skolemization)
Skolemize: Replace ∃ with Skolem functions/constants
Distribute ∀ inward, Convert to CNF (AND of ORs)
Flatten nested clauses, Convert to set of clauses
For ∀x (P(x) → Q(x)): = ∀x (¬P(x) ∨ Q(x))
Clause form: {¬P(x), Q(x)} or equivalently P(x) → Q(x) as single clause

questions.question: Given KB: (1) ∀x (Man(x) → Mortal(x)) (2) Man(Socrates). Prove that Mortal(Socrates) using (a) Modus Ponens (b) Resolution refutation.
keyPoints: (a) Modus Ponens approach: From ∀x (Man(x)→Mortal(x)) and Man(Socrates), apply Universal Instantiation to get Man(Socrates)→Mortal(Socrates), then Modus Ponens gives Mortal(Socrates)
(b) Resolution refutation: Add ¬Mortal(Socrates) to KB
Clauses: {¬Man(x), Mortal(x)}, {Man(Socrates)}, {¬Mortal(Socrates)}
Resolve {¬Man(x), Mortal(x)} with {¬Mortal(Socrates)} to get {¬Man(Socrates)}
Resolve {¬Man(Socrates)} with {Man(Socrates)} to get empty clause □
Empty clause derived → contradiction → ¬Mortal(Socrates) unsatisfiable → Mortal(Socrates) entailed
